title: Efficient LLM Inference: Bandwidth, Compute, Synchronization, and Capacity are all you need
content_excerpt:
* [4.4Role of Bandwidth on System Performance](https://arxiv.org/html/2507.14397v1#S4.SS4 "In 4 Results ‣ Efficient LLM Inference: Bandwidth, Compute, Synchronization, and Capacity are all you need")

LLM Serving. LLM inference can be broadly divided into two stages: the prefill phase and the decode phase. The prefill phase processes the initial prompt or context, generating intermediate representations of the prompt tokens that are used in the subsequent decode phase. The decode phase, which is auto-regressive, generates one token at a time, conditioned on the previously generated tokens (Figure[1](https://arxiv.org/html/2507.14397v1#S2.F1 "Figure 1 ‣ 2 Modeling ‣ Efficient LLM Inference: Bandwidth, Compute, Synchronization, and Capacity are all you need")shows a tranformer layer for Llama3-70B). In modern deployment scenarios, it is common to have a separate prefill server or cluster and a decode server[10.5555/3691938.3691949](https://arxiv.org/html/2507.14397v1#bib.bib51);[10609649](https://arxiv.org/html/2507.14397v1#bib.bib27), to optimize for each phase. DeepSeekV3’s inference deployment provisions10×10\times10 ×more nodes for decode compared to prefill.Since the decode phase dominates the execution time for many applications, it is the primary focus of this limit study.
Our next step is to understand the level of performance feasible with a “baseline” xPU-HBM3 system. To study this, we sweep the system scale from 1 to 128 chips, and for each system, we spread each of the operators across all the chips, and apply reductions as necessary when partial sums are computed in each chip: thus adding synchronization delays. As system scale increases, the aggregate bandwidth and capacity increases allowing higher user- and system- token throughput. This comes at the cost of increasing amounts of “exposed latency” - grouping together more chips causes longer latencies. Table[2](https://arxiv.org/html/2507.14397v1#S4.T2 "Table 2 ‣ 4.2 Performance and Efficiency from Scaling xPU Systems ‣ 4 Results ‣ Efficient LLM Inference: Bandwidth, Compute, Synchronization, and Capacity are all you need")shows the performance of the 3 models at 4K and 128K context lengths. As expected from the AMI results from above, the chips’ performance is correlated with the amount of bandwidth they provide. For a given model and given context, bigger systems provide more bandwidth and hence higher performance. For a given model and given system, increasing the context length, puts more pressure on bandwidth slightly reducing token throughput. We show expanded results for various context lengths in Appendix[B](https://arxiv.org/html/2507.14397v1#A2 "Appendix B Detailed Results ‣ Efficient LLM Inference: Bandwidth, Compute, Synchronization, and Capacity are all you need").
We now analyze how muchbandwidth improvement alonecan increase performance. To isolate this effect, we start with the largest system: xPU-HBM3-TP128, and configure it to have a very low exposed synchronization latencies, by settingTT⁢P⁢S⁢y⁢n⁢csubscript𝑇𝑇𝑃𝑆𝑦𝑛𝑐T_{TPSync}italic_T start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_T italic_P italic_S italic_y italic_n italic_c end_POSTSUBSCRIPTto 200ns. We then vary the memory bandwidth from 4TB/sec up to 120 TB/sec (roughly the limit of what’s achievable with on-chip SRAM on one die). We normalize performance (UTPS) to that of xPU-HBM3-TP128. Figure[2](https://arxiv.org/html/2507.14397v1#S4.F2 "Figure 2 ‣ 4.4 Role of Bandwidth on System Performance ‣ 4 Results ‣ Efficient LLM Inference: Bandwidth, Compute, Synchronization, and Capacity are all you need")shows the results for 3 different context sizes and 3 models. The improvements show an asymptotic curve with rapid increase early on. As more and more bandwidth is added, relatively more fraction of the time is spent in the exposed communication latency portion of the workload, which leads to improvements tapering off.
